questions.stem: Epidemiology is best defined as the study of which of the following?
options: The distribution and determinants of health and disease in populations.
The internal anatomy of a single patient.
questions.explanation: Epidemiology is the study of the distribution and determinants of health-related states and events, such as disease, in defined populations. It asks who is affected, where, and when, and what factors influence the pattern. Its focus on populations distinguishes it from clinical medicine, which centres on individual patients.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: The incidence of a disease measures which quantity?
options: The number of new cases arising in a population over a period of time.
The total number of existing cases at one point in time.
The number of people who recover.
questions.explanation: Incidence measures the number of new cases of a disease that arise in a population at risk over a defined period. It captures the rate at which people are newly affected. This contrasts with prevalence, which counts all existing cases at a given moment.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: The prevalence of a disease measures which quantity?
options: Only the new cases this week.
The total number of existing cases in a population at a given time.
questions.explanation: Prevalence measures the total number of existing cases, both old and new, present in a population at a particular point in time, divided by the population. It reflects the burden of disease at that moment. Unlike incidence, it includes cases that began before the period of interest.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which statement correctly contrasts incidence and prevalence?
options: Incidence counts deaths, while prevalence counts births.
Incidence and prevalence are the same measure.
Prevalence counts only new cases, while incidence counts old cases.
Incidence counts new cases over a period, while prevalence counts all existing cases at a point in time.
questions.explanation: Incidence is about new cases occurring over a span of time, capturing the rate of new disease, whereas prevalence is about all cases that exist at a single point, capturing the total burden. A chronic disease that lasts a long time can have low incidence but high prevalence. Keeping the two apart is fundamental in epidemiology.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: The attack rate in an outbreak is best described as which of the following?
options: The proportion of an exposed or at-risk group that develops the disease during the outbreak.
The speed at which a person walks.
The total population of a country.
questions.explanation: The attack rate is the proportion of an exposed or at-risk group that becomes ill during a defined outbreak period. It is a form of cumulative incidence used especially for short, sharp outbreaks such as foodborne illness. It is usually expressed as a percentage of those at risk.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: At a function, 200 people ate a particular meal and 50 of them later fell ill with food poisoning. What is the attack rate?
questions.explanation: The attack rate is the number who fell ill divided by the number at risk, $50 / 200 = 0.25$, which is 25 percent. The denominator is the 200 people who ate the meal, since they form the exposed group. Multiplying the proportion by 100 converts it to a percentage.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: At a wedding, 80 guests ate the buffet and 24 developed diarrhoea afterwards. What is the attack rate?
questions.explanation: The attack rate is $24 / 80 = 0.30$, which is 30 percent. The 80 guests who ate the buffet are the at-risk denominator, and the 24 who became ill are the numerator. The result is a cumulative incidence over the outbreak.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: In a town of 1500 people initially free of a disease, 30 new cases arise during one year. What is the incidence, expressed per 1000 people per year?
questions.explanation: The incidence proportion is $30 / 1500 = 0.02$, and multiplying by 1000 gives 20 new cases per 1000 people per year. The denominator is the population at risk of 1500, and the numerator is the 30 new cases. Expressing rates per 1000 makes small proportions easier to read.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: In a community of 900 people, 45 currently have a chronic illness. What is the prevalence, expressed as a percentage?
questions.explanation: Prevalence is the number of existing cases divided by the population, $45 / 900 = 0.05$, which is 5 percent. All current cases are counted in the numerator, regardless of when they began. Multiplying the proportion by 100 gives the percentage.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: In a population of 5000 people, 200 are living with a particular condition at a given time. What is the prevalence per 1000 people?
questions.explanation: Prevalence is $200 / 5000 = 0.04$, and multiplying by 1000 gives 40 cases per 1000 people. This counts all existing cases at the chosen time, divided by the whole population. The figure expresses the current burden of the condition.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: In epidemiology, morbidity refers to which of the following?
options: The occurrence of illness or disease in a population.
The occurrence of death in a population.
questions.explanation: Morbidity refers to the presence of illness or disease in a population, that is, how many people are sick. Measures such as incidence and prevalence quantify morbidity. It is distinct from mortality, which deals with deaths.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: In epidemiology, mortality refers to which of the following?
options: The occurrence of illness without death.
The occurrence of death in a population.
questions.explanation: Mortality refers to death in a population, and the mortality rate measures the frequency of deaths relative to the population over a period. It is contrasted with morbidity, which deals with illness rather than death. Both are key summary measures of population health.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A disease that is constantly present at a relatively steady, usually low, level in a particular area is described as which of the following?
questions.explanation: An endemic disease is one that is constantly present at a relatively steady baseline level in a given area or population. Malaria in many tropical regions is a classic example. An epidemic is a sharp rise above this expected level, and a pandemic is an epidemic spread over many countries.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A sudden increase in the number of cases of a disease above the expected level in a community is described as which of the following?
questions.explanation: An epidemic is the occurrence of cases of a disease clearly in excess of what is normally expected in a community or region. It signals an outbreak rising above the endemic baseline. When such an excess spreads across many countries or continents, it is instead called a pandemic.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: An epidemic that has spread over several countries or continents, affecting a very large number of people, is described as which of the following?
questions.explanation: A pandemic is an epidemic that has spread across several countries or continents and affects a very large number of people. It is essentially an epidemic on a global scale. An endemic disease, by contrast, stays at a steady level within a particular area.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which sequence correctly orders these terms from the smallest to the largest geographic scale of disease occurrence?
questions.explanation: An endemic disease sits at a steady level in one area, an epidemic is an excess of cases within a community or region, and a pandemic spreads across many countries or continents. Ordered by increasing geographic scale, this gives endemic, then epidemic, then pandemic. The terms describe how widely the disease is occurring relative to expectation.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: In a cohort study, how are participants typically grouped and followed?
options: They are grouped by their exposure status and followed forward in time to see who develops the disease.
They are grouped by whether they already have the disease and then asked about past exposures.
They are measured only once, at a single point in time.
They are never followed over time.
questions.explanation: In a cohort study, participants are classified by their exposure status, exposed or unexposed, and then followed forward in time to observe who develops the disease. This design allows incidence to be compared between the groups. It contrasts with a case-control study, which starts from disease status and looks backward at exposures.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: In a case-control study, how are participants typically selected?
options: By their exposure status, then followed forward in time.
By whether they have the disease, comparing cases with controls and looking back at past exposures.
By measuring the whole population only once.
By choosing only healthy young adults.
questions.explanation: A case-control study starts by selecting people who already have the disease, the cases, and a comparison group without it, the controls, then looks backward to compare their past exposures. This design is efficient for rare diseases. It differs from a cohort study, which begins with exposure and follows participants forward.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A cross-sectional study examines a population in which way?
options: It assigns people at random to a treatment.
It studies only those who have died.
It measures exposure and disease at a single point in time, like a snapshot.
It follows people for many years into the future.
questions.explanation: A cross-sectional study assesses exposure and disease together at a single point in time, providing a snapshot of the population. It is well suited to estimating prevalence. Because it does not follow people over time, it cannot easily establish whether exposure preceded disease.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A defining feature of a randomized controlled trial is which of the following?
options: Participants are assigned to treatment or control groups by chance.
questions.explanation: In a randomized controlled trial, participants are assigned to the treatment or the control group by a random process, which helps balance other factors between the groups. This random allocation is what makes the trial a powerful tool for testing whether an intervention works. Letting participants choose their own group would introduce bias.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: The germ theory of disease causation holds which of the following?
options: Disease is caused only by bad air, or miasma.
Disease is a punishment with no physical cause.
questions.explanation: The germ theory of disease causation holds that specific microorganisms are the causes of specific infectious diseases. It replaced older ideas such as the miasma theory, which blamed bad air. The germ theory underpins much of modern epidemiology and infection control.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: The older miasma theory of disease, later largely replaced by the germ theory, attributed disease to which cause?
questions.explanation: The miasma theory attributed disease to miasma, meaning bad air or noxious vapours thought to arise from rotting matter. It was an influential idea before the germ theory. The work of Pasteur, Koch, and others showed instead that microorganisms cause many diseases, largely replacing the miasma view.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which study design is generally considered the strongest for testing whether a new treatment actually causes an improvement?
options: A case report of one patient.
questions.explanation: A randomized controlled trial is generally considered the strongest design for testing whether a treatment causes an effect, because random allocation balances confounding factors between groups. This makes any difference in outcome more credibly due to the treatment itself. Cross-sectional studies and single case reports provide weaker evidence of causation.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which statement about a disease with a long duration, such as a chronic condition, is most accurate?
options: Its incidence must always equal its prevalence.
It can have no prevalence at all.
Its prevalence must always be lower than its incidence.
It can have a low incidence but a high prevalence.
questions.explanation: Because prevalence reflects both how often new cases occur and how long they last, a long-lasting chronic disease can accumulate many existing cases even if few new ones arise each year. Such a disease can therefore have a low incidence yet a high prevalence. Incidence and prevalence are only equal in special circumstances, not in general.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: At a gathering, 80 people ate a suspect dish and 24 of them later developed gastroenteritis. Work out the attack rate.
questions.solution: The attack rate is the number who became ill divided by the number at risk, $24 / 80$, which as a decimal equals {{1}}. Expressed as a percentage, this attack rate is {{2}} percent.
questions.explanation: The attack rate is $24 / 80 = 0.30$, which is 30 percent. The 80 exposed people form the denominator and the 24 ill people the numerator.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: In a town of 1500 people who are initially disease-free, 30 new cases of an illness arise over one year. Work out the incidence.
questions.solution: The incidence proportion is the number of new cases divided by the population at risk, $30 / 1500$, which equals {{1}} as a decimal. Multiplying by 1000, the incidence is {{2}} new cases per 1000 people per year.
questions.explanation: The incidence proportion is $30 / 1500 = 0.02$, and multiplying by 1000 gives 20 new cases per 1000 people per year. The denominator is the at-risk population of 1500.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: In a community of 900 people, 45 individuals currently have a particular chronic illness. Work out the prevalence.
questions.solution: The prevalence is the number of existing cases divided by the population, $45 / 900$, which equals {{1}} as a decimal. Expressed as a percentage, the prevalence is {{2}} percent.
questions.explanation: The prevalence is $45 / 900 = 0.05$, which is 5 percent. All current cases are counted, divided by the whole population of 900.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Match each epidemiological term to its meaning by choosing the correct option for each blank.
questions.solution: A measure of the number of new cases arising over a period is called {{1}}. A disease that is constantly present at a steady level in a particular area is described as {{2}}. A study in which participants are assigned to treatment or control groups by chance is a {{3}} controlled trial.
questions.explanation: Incidence counts new cases over a period, an endemic disease is steadily present in an area, and a randomized controlled trial assigns participants to groups by chance.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)
